proposal.reasoning: This is the first iteration in the exploration phase with no prior data. I am proposing 4 diverse parameter sets to map the response surface of the force field. The strategy involves: (1) Fork 1 'Strong Cohesion': High epsilon values to test if stronger LJ interactions can increase density and HVap; (2) Fork 2 'Weak/Large': Low epsilon and large rmin to test sensitivity to packing; (3) Fork 3 'High Polarity': Maximum charges and long dummy bonds to maximize dipole moment toward the target 3.72 D; (4) Fork 4 'Tight Packing': Small rmin with moderate epsilon to test density optimization via packing efficiency. All forks respect physical constraints: dummy bond lengths < sigma/2 of core beads, dummy force constants > 100, and charges are equal and opposite.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Changing LJ epsilon (CGD2_CNI_epsilon, CNI_MOC_epsilon) from weak (-0.9) to strong (-1.5) will modulate cohesive energy, directly affecting density, heat of vaporization, and surface tension because stronger dispersion interactions lead to tighter packing and higher energy barriers for vaporization. Changing rmin parameters (CNI_rminby2, MOC_rminby2) from small (2.3-2.8) to large (2.6-3.2) will alter packing efficiency and free volume. Changing DUP/DUN charges (±0.25 to ±0.45) and bond lengths (CNI_DUP_bl: 1.0-1.6, MOC_DUN_bl: 0.8-1.4) will control the molecular dipole moment through μ = q × d, where increasing charge magnitude and separation distance increases dipole moment toward the target 3.72 D. Angle variations (theta0) adjust the vector orientation of the dummy beads affecting the net dipole.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will identify whether the system requires stronger cohesion (higher epsilon) or tighter packing (smaller rmin) to achieve target density (0.936 g/cm³) and HVap (10.95 kcal/mol). hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel molecular dynamics simulations, measuring density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment to identify which combination of cohesive strength, packing efficiency, and electrostatic parameters best reproduces experimental targets.

proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic indicates the system suffers from insufficient density (7-8% below target) due to excessive excluded volume from large Rmin values, while also showing slightly elevated surface tension and heat of vaporization. Following the recommendations, I propose 4 parallel strategies: (1) Conservative adjustment following diagnostic guidance with moderate Rmin reduction (5-7%), charge reduction to ±0.22, and dummy bond elongation to maintain dipole; (2) Aggressive geometric compaction with Rmin values at minimum boundaries to maximize density increase; (3) Aggressive electronic optimization with larger charge reduction (±0.20) compensated by maximum dummy bond lengths to minimize surface tension while preserving dipole; (4) Combined geometric and cohesive energy adjustment with moderate Rmin reduction and reduced epsilon magnitudes (except CNI_MOC_epsilon which is maintained at -0.5 per recommendations) to address the thermodynamic over-stabilization. This explores the trade-off between density (requiring smaller Rmin) and surface tension (requiring weaker cohesion/polarity), testing whether charge/bond compensation can offset the surface tension increase from higher density packing.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing Rmin parameters by 5-10% decreases excluded volume, allowing tighter molecular packing to increase density from 0.869 toward the target 0.936 g/cm³. Simultaneously, reducing dummy bead charge magnitudes while increasing dummy bond lengths (CNI_DUP_bl and MOC_DUN_bl) maintains the q×r dipole product near 3.72 D while reducing excessive polarity that contributes to high surface tension. In Fork 4, additionally reducing epsilon magnitudes (making them less negative) decreases van der Waals cohesion to address the elevated heat of vaporization and surface tension, while keeping CNI_MOC_epsilon at -0.5 as it provides optimal density response.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This multi-pronged approach will increase density toward target by geometric compaction (smaller Rmin), while the charge/bond compensation strategy prevents surface tension from increasing excessively during densification. The dipole moment will remain accurate at ~3.72 D through the inverse relationship between charge magnitude and bond length. Fork 4 specifically tests whether modest reduction in epsilon can fine-tune the cohesive energy without compromising the density gains.
hypothesis.test_method: Evaluate all 4 parameter sets in parallel, monitoring for density convergence to 0.936 g/cm³, surface tension reduction toward 32.4 dyn/cm, and maintenance of dipole moment at 3.72 D. Check that dummy bond lengths remain within sigma/2 constraints of their parent beads and that simulations remain stable without velocity overflows.

proposal.reasoning: Based on the diagnostic report, the system shows a classic trade-off: density is 1.8-2.1% too low (needs tighter packing), while surface tension is 5.2-7.5% too high (needs weaker cohesion). The dipole moment is also below target (3.28-3.67 vs 3.72 D). Following the diagnostic recommendations: (1) I will increase |DUP_charge| from 0.22 toward 0.24-0.28 to boost dipole moment without relying on bond elongation (which the diagnostic warns against); (2) Decrease Rmin values by 0.1-0.2 Å to increase density through reduced excluded volume; (3) Decrease epsilon magnitudes by 0.02-0.08 to reduce surface tension and heat of vaporization, counteracting the cohesion increase from tighter packing and higher charges. Four diverse strategies are proposed: (Fork 1) Balanced approach with moderate charge increase (0.25) and uniform Rmin reduction; (Fork 2) Aggressive electronic optimization with maximum charge (0.28), minimum Rmin values, and strongest epsilon reduction to test dipole limits while controlling ST; (Fork 3) Geometric compaction focus with minimum Rmin values and moderate charge (0.24), maintaining current dummy bond lengths; (Fork 4) Asymmetric adjustment reducing CGD2_CNI_rmin and CGD2_MOC_rmin aggressively while keeping CNI_MOC_rmin moderate (4.6) to test which interaction dominates the density/ST trade-off. All forks maintain dummy bond lengths ≤ current values (1.2/1.0) per diagnostic guidance.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Increasing DUP/DUN charges from ±0.22 to ±0.24-0.28 will increase the molecular dipole moment toward the target of 3.72 D by enhancing the charge separation contribution (μ = q × d). Simultaneously, reducing Lennard-Jones Rmin parameters (CGD2_CNI_rmin, CNI_MOC_rmin, CGD2_MOC_rmin) by 0.05-0.2 Å decreases excluded volume, allowing tighter molecular packing to increase density from 0.918 toward 0.936 g/cm³. To counteract the expected increase in surface tension and heat of vaporization from both higher charges (stronger electrostatics) and tighter packing (increased van der Waals interactions at shorter distances), we reduce epsilon magnitudes (particularly CNI_MOC_epsilon from -0.5 toward -0.4 to -0.45) to weaken cohesive forces, directly addressing the observed 7.5% excess in surface tension. Dummy bond lengths are maintained at or slightly below current values (1.1-1.2 Å) following diagnostic guidance that geometric elongation does not effectively increase dipole in this regime and may destabilize the model.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will improve density toward 0.936 g/cm³ by geometric compaction, reduce surface tension toward 32.43 dyn/cm and heat of vaporization toward 10.951 kcal/mol by reducing epsilon magnitudes, and increase dipole moment toward 3.72 D through enhanced charge magnitudes, resolving the primary deviations identified in the diagnostic.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel simulations, monitoring for convergence of density, surface tension, heat of vaporization, and dipole moment to their respective targets while ensuring temperature dependence remains physically correct (decreasing with temperature).

proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic indicates the current best parameter set (score 13.56) has excellent density agreement but suffers from excessive cohesive energy (HVap 5-6% too high, ST slightly elevated) and overestimated dipole moment (charges at ±0.25 are too high). Since we are in the exploration phase with consistent improvement (scores dropping from 20.5 to 13.56), I propose 4 diverse strategies that follow the diagnostic recommendations: (1) Balanced global reduction of charges and epsilon values; (2) Aggressive epsilon reduction with maximum charge decrease to test the lower bounds; (3) Geometric contraction of dummy bonds combined with moderate electronic adjustments to test alternative dipole control mechanisms; (4) Selective reduction of only CNI_MOC_epsilon (the dominant interaction) with minimal charge reduction to isolate the effect of specific interaction strengths. All forks maintain Rmin values to preserve the excellent density agreement and respect dummy bond constraints (< Rmin/2).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing DUP/DUN charges from ±0.25 to ±0.22-0.24 will decrease the molecular dipole moment toward the target 3.72 D by reducing the charge separation contribution (μ = q × d). Simultaneously, making epsilon parameters less negative (particularly CNI_MOC_epsilon from -0.45 toward -0.38 to -0.42, and others from -0.28 toward -0.22 to -0.26) will weaken van der Waals attractions, thereby reducing the excessive heat of vaporization and surface tension while maintaining density (since Rmin values are unchanged). Fork 3 additionally tests bond length contraction (5% reduction) as an alternative geometric mechanism to fine-tune the dipole moment if charge reduction alone proves insufficient.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will simultaneously correct the dipole moment overestimation and reduce the thermodynamic over-stabilization (high HVap/ST) toward their respective targets, potentially achieving a score below 10 by better balancing electrostatic and dispersive interactions.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel, monitoring for convergence of dipole moment to 3.72 D, reduction of HVap toward 10.95 kcal/mol and ST toward 32.4/31.6 dyn/cm, while maintaining density accuracy within 0.2%.

proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic report reveals systematic underestimation of cohesive properties: density (-1.4 to -1.8%), HVap (-2.6 to -3.0%), and especially surface tension (-5.0% at 298K, -10.4% at 313K). Simultaneously, the dipole moment is consistently overestimated. Following the diagnostic recommendations, I propose 4 parallel strategies: (1) Increase epsilon magnitudes to -0.32 for non-bonded interactions while contracting dummy bond lengths to reduce dipole; (2) Reduce charges to ±0.23 to address the high dipole while moderately increasing epsilons; (3) Combine slight Rmin reduction with epsilon increase to -0.33 for density optimization; (4) Test aggressive epsilon increase to -0.35 with the diagnostic-suggested CNI_MOC_epsilon of -0.38 to isolate the effect of maximum cohesion boost. This explores the trade-off between enhancing intermolecular attraction (requiring larger epsilon magnitudes) and correcting the electronic properties (requiring reduced charge separation).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Increasing LJ epsilon magnitudes from -0.28 toward -0.32 to -0.35 strengthens intermolecular cohesive forces (van der Waals attraction), which directly increases density (tighter packing), heat of vaporization (stronger intermolecular bonds to break), and surface tension (higher cohesive energy density) per the parameter-property relationships. Simultaneously, reducing dummy bead bond lengths (CNI_DUP_bl, MOC_DUN_bl) from 1.15/0.95 toward 1.08-1.12/0.88-0.92 reduces the charge separation distance (d in μ=q×d), thereby lowering the dipole moment toward the 3.72 D target without requiring charge reduction that would compromise electrostatic contributions to surface tension. Minor Rmin reductions (0.02 Å) further enhance density by reducing excluded volume.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will simultaneously correct the three underestimated thermodynamic properties (density, HVap, ST) by enhancing intermolecular cohesion while bringing the overestimated dipole moment down to target. The 313K surface tension, which shows the largest error and highest parameter sensitivity, should show particular improvement toward the 31.56 dyn/cm target.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel molecular dynamics simulations, monitoring convergence of density to 0.936/0.924 g/cm³, HVap to 10.951/10.743 kcal/mol, ST to 32.43/31.56 dyn/cm, and dipole moment to 3.72 D, with special attention to the temperature dependence of surface tension.
